content.assessment: This proposal engages legal authority and accountability most squarely of all options, since it responds directly to a decade-plus of unresolved ECtHR judgments and ongoing Committee of Ministers monitoring, plus documented ombudsman findings that segregation complaints dominate current caseloads. Extending anti-segregation safeguards to free school choice and selective tracks would require new legal authority over admissions and institutional autonomy domains not currently regulated for this purpose, directly implicating maintainer autonomy (public, private, and church-school operators) and raising enforceability questions given the acknowledged absence of systematic ethnic-disaggregated data to monitor compliance.
opportunities: Directly addresses long-standing Council of Europe and EU accountability exposure, potentially resolving compliance gaps open since 2013
strengths: Directly responsive to outstanding, legally binding international obligations (ECtHR judgments, Committee of Ministers monitoring) rather than treating segregation as a peripheral concern
Cross-domain coordination mechanism could institutionalize anticipatory legal/governance review, using the 2020 readiness-rule precedent as a working model
threats: Documented institutional resistance since at least 2013 (Bridge classes precedent) suggests high likelihood of resistance to structural reform of admissions and school-choice rules
Middle-class exit to private/church schools may continue independent of any administrative coordination mechanism, limiting real-world enforceability even if legal authority is established
weaknesses: Absence of systematic ethnically-disaggregated data collection undermines the enforceability of any new anti-segregation safeguard, since compliance cannot be reliably monitored
Extending safeguards to selective/private/church tracks raises significant maintainer-autonomy conflicts not resolved by the proposal's mechanisms
